title: One Marriage under God: The Campaign to Promote Marriage in America By Melanie Heath New York University Press. 2012. 273 pages. $75.00 cloth, $24.00 paper
abstract: In this impressive ethnography, Heath examines the implementation of marriage promotion in Oklahoma. Oklahoma was the first state to set aside a significant amount of its Temporary Assistance to Needy Families (TANF) funding to promote marriage in 1999, and has since become a “vanguard” for marriage promotion across the nation. She explores the ideology of marriage promotion, which brings together cultural repertoires of the middle-class, married, heterosexual family as the model of American citizenship, and the social consequences of efforts to re-institutionalize “one marriage.” The research is based on participant observations of a wide variety of marriage promotion workshops as well as in-depth interviews with participants and leaders. Since the marriage movement first emerged in the United States, divorce rates have steadily declined, but the anxieties that drive the need to promote marriage remain, fueled in large part by efforts to legalize same-sex marriage. Heath convincingly presents the ideological connections between marriage promotion and campaigns against same-sex marriage. She makes it clear that the same “boundary work” motivates efforts to encourage poor single mothers to marry and to exclude gay men and lesbians from the institution. While others have argued that marriage is used as a tool by the State to produce the “right kind” of citizenry, Heath's work shows readers how this is achieved on the ground. She empirically demonstrates that the marriage movement is just as much about regulating gender, sexuality, class, and race as it is about promoting marriage.
